answer:
**In "Swami Ram Tirath" (Bang-e-Dara Part 2, chapter 23), Allama Iqbal pays tribute to the Hindu sage (1861–1906, a Vedantic mystic who renounced worldly life) through a short Sufi-inspired poem that celebrates his spiritual transformation and enlightenment.**[^1]

The poem addresses Ram Tirath directly as a "restless drop" that merges into the divine "river's embrace," evolving from a mere pearl into one "beyond price"—symbolizing fana' (ego-annihilation) and union with the Absolute.[^2] Iqbal marvels at how the sage "unveiled the secret of color and scent" (transcending sensory illusions), while the poet confesses to remaining "a prisoner of their distinctions."[^3] It evokes Ram Tirath's achievement of mystical negation ("the river of 'La'" hiding the pearl of divine unity, "illa-Allah"), where life's end becomes eternal tumult, a spark turns into a cosmic fire, and love's ecstasy shatters the self-idol—like Abraham smashing idols.[^4]

This cross-traditional homage blends Vedanta's self-dissolution with Iqbal's Islamic/Sufi khudi (selfhood) reversal, positioning Ram Tirath as an exemplar of transcendent realization beyond reason.[^5]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, ch. 23 "Swami Ram Tirath" ([translation](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/23-swami-ram-tirath/translation/)).

[^2]: Ibid., [p-d1bdd5](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/23-swami-ram-tirath/translation/#p-d1bdd5).

[^3]: Ibid., [p-cb1e8e](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/23-swami-ram-tirath/translation/#p-cb1e8e).

[^4]: Ibid., [¶¶ p-420405, p-ef7cac, p-7de605](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/23-swami-ram-tirath/translation/#p-420405#p-ef7cac#p-7de605).

[^5]: Ibid., full chapter ([translation](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/23-swami-ram-tirath/translation/)).
